topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. I reread the subject line three times, my thumb hovering over the 'delete' button before finally clicking on the body of the message. The news was devastating: Northwood University was ceasing operations at the end of the semester. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like an icy tendril. I walked to my dorm room, the familiar posters on the wall suddenly feeling mocking in their cheerfulness. I stared out at the campus quad, the vibrant autumn colors seeming muted, washed out by an impending grayness. I paced back and forth, my shoes scuffing the worn carpet, unable to settle. Every sound—the distant chatter of students, the rustle of leaves—seemed amplified, each one a reminder of something slipping away. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but the words caught in my throat. What was I supposed to say? The silence in my small room was deafening, pressing in on me.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this?" I asked, the words tasting dry and brittle in my mouth. Dr. Albright’s voice on the phone was measured, professional, but beneath the surface, I could hear the tremor of finality. I gripped the phone so tightly my knuckles turned white. When the confirmation came through, it felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I stood by the window, watching the afternoon light slant across the empty lecture halls. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I started pacing the length of my small apartment, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I kept running my hands over my face, feeling a strange heat rising beneath my skin. The future, which I had meticulously mapped out in terms of graduation ceremonies and future job prospects, had just dissolved into vapor. I sank onto the edge of my bed, pulling my knees to my chest, a low, involuntary sound escaping my lips—a soft, ragged exhale.

[story 3]

I kept glancing at the bulletin board in the student union, half-expecting a last-minute announcement, a reprieve, anything to disrupt the suffocating certainty that had settled over me. Nothing. Just the usual faded flyers and the peeling paint. A deep sense of disorientation washed over me, as if the ground beneath my feet had suddenly tilted. I tried to focus on my textbook, but the words swam before my eyes, refusing to anchor themselves. I pushed the book away, the sound sharp in the quiet hallway. My hands began to shake, a subtle vibration that spread up my arms. I walked to the common area, leaning against a wall, just trying to anchor myself to something solid. The air felt too thin, and a tightness constricted my chest, making each inhale feel like a deliberate, difficult effort. I just stood there, letting the sheer scale of the situation wash over me, a heavy, immobilizing pressure.
